title: Checklist
description: A factory planner checklist is a live outcomes and workstream board that shows done, active, ready-next, and intentionally held work.
openingSummary: A checklist is the factory planner's live board of customer outcomes and workstreams. It shows what is done, active, ready next, and intentionally held—without treating phase names as mandatory gates.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A checklist is a live outcomes and workstream board used by the factory planner. It shows customer outcomes alongside workstreams in four states: done, active, ready next, and intentionally held. Phase names may appear as optional labels, but they are not mandatory gates that force every stream to wait for a global stage to finish.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: A checklist lets you track customer outcomes, see which workstreams can move in parallel, and record real holds with concrete reasons. Without that board, finished work, ready work, and blocked work collapse into one vague queue, so planners slow safe streams and miss what the customer still needs.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Picture a compact board for a docs rewrite. Customer outcomes: publish the install guide and the getting-started page. Done: the install guide lane is finished. Active: the checklist concept page is in progress. Ready next: the task-queue concept page can start when a lane frees up. Held: a colliding registry change is already in flight, so that lane waits for a concrete collision reason—not because a phase label says later work must wait.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: In factory planning, the checklist usually lives as planner-owned state such as a live checklist file. It sits beside a lightweight queue summary and an append-only planner log so active, ready, held, and completed workstreams stay visible across sessions.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: A checklist is not a task queue. A task queue is ordered work waiting to run; a checklist is the live outcomes and workstream board that shows done, active, ready next, and held status across streams. A checklist is also not an architectural checklist or a review acceptance-criteria list—those are static verification lists you tick once. And it is not a phase-gated project plan that forces every stream to finish one global phase before the next can start.
